2: ein qadosh kaYHWH ki ein biltecha vein tzur kebogheinu: 'There is none holy like YHWH: for there is none besides you; there is no rock like our God.' Hannah's prayer is the OT's most concentrated statement of YHWH's incomparable holiness and sovereignty — expressed through the reversal-of-fortunes theme (vv. 4-8: the bows of the mighty are broken, but the feeble bind on strength). The pattern is: YHWH exalts the lowly and humbles the proud, not based on human merit but on divine grace and election. Paul's 'God chose what is weak in the world to shame the strong' (1 Cor 1:27) is the Christological application of Hannah's insight: the cross itself is YHWH's ultimate reversal, where the weak and crucified Son defeats the strong.
1: Hannah's prayer opens: ʿālaz libbî bYHWH rāmāh qarnî bYHWH — my heart exults in YHWH; my horn is exalted in YHWH. ʿālaz (H5937, exult intensely — used in Ps 28:7; Zeph 3:14) connotes fierce, fighting joy. qeren (H7161, horn) — the horn of a bull or ram as the seat of its power; 'my horn is exalted' means my strength/dignity/honor has been lifted. Hannah's prayer closes (v10) with wĕyārēm qeren mĕšîḥô (he will exalt the horn of his anointed) — the same rûm qeren formula. The opening personal exaltation and the closing messianic exaltation are connected: Hannah's lifted horn is the prototype of the ultimate horn-exaltation. Mary's Magnificat (Luke 1:46-55) expands this prayer, applying the reversals to the coming of the Messiah.
3: 'Do not speak with such great pride' — ʾal-tarḇû tĕḏabbĕrû gĕḇōhāh gĕḇōhāh: the doubled gĕḇōhāh (H1364, high/proud) is an intensifying repetition. 'YHWH is a God of knowledges' (ʾēl dēʿôt YHWH) — the plural dēʿôt (knowledges) indicates exhaustive or multidimensional knowledge. 'And by him actions are weighed' (wĕlô niṯkĕnû ʿălilôt) — tāḵan (H8254, to weigh/assess) is used in Prov 16:2; 21:2 for YHWH's weighing of human spirits. The sovereignty of YHWH's knowledge grounds the prohibition of arrogance: one cannot boast before the One who weighs every action in his comprehensive, plural knowledge.
4: 'The bows of the mighty are shattered, but those who stumbled are girded with strength' — qešet gibbōrîm ḥattîm wĕniḵšālîm ʾāzĕrû ḥāyil. gibbōrîm (H1368, mighty warriors) are those who rely on their military strength; their bows (qešet, the premier long-range weapon) are 'shattered' (ḥattat, H2865 — the same root as 'terror/dismay' that overcomes enemies). niḵšālîm (those who stumbled, H3782) — the visibly vulnerable who are then 'girded with strength' (ʾāzar ḥayil). The pattern — strong rendered powerless, stumbling made strong — is the theological reversal Paul applies in 1 Cor 1:27-29 and that the cross itself enacts.
5: 'The barren woman has borne seven, but she who has many children languishes' (ʿăqārāh yāleḏāh šiḇʿāh wĕraḇbat bānîm ʾumlālāh). The seven children (the perfect, complete number — seven as fullness) born to the formerly barren is the direct application to Hannah: she was barren; YHWH will give her five more children (v21) approaching the prayer's 'seven.' The 'many-son woman' (raḇbat bānîm) — Peninnah — who now 'languishes' (ʾumlālāh, H535, wilts/weakens). The prayer is simultaneously a theological statement about YHWH's reversals and a personal testimony about Hannah's own experience — and a prophecy about what is coming for Peninnah's position of advantage.
6: 'YHWH kills and makes alive; he brings down to Sheol and raises up' (YHWH mēmît ûmĕḥayyeh môrîḏ šĕʾôl wayaʿal). This is the OT's most direct paired statement of divine sovereignty over life and death before Deut 32:39. šĕʾôl (H7585) — the realm of the dead, conceived as below the earth (Job 10:21-22; Ps 88:3-6). YHWH's power to 'bring up' from Sheol (wayyaʿal, to ascend/bring up) is the background for NT resurrection language. Deut 32:39 parallels exactly: 'I kill and make alive.' Both texts assert the same monotheistic sovereignty: YHWH alone controls the boundary between life and death — no competing power shares this authority.
7: 'He raises up the poor from the dust, lifts the needy from the ash heap' (mĕqîm mēʿāfār dāl mēʾašpōt yārîm ʾeḇyôn). ʿāfār (H6083, dust/earth) — the lowest position, associated with mourning (Job 2:12) and humiliation (Isa 47:1). ʾašpōt (H830, ash-heap/dung-heap/refuse pile) — where the destitute and sick sat (Job 2:8). Ps 113:7-8 quotes these phrases almost verbatim: 'He raises the poor from the dust and lifts the needy from the ash heap, to make them sit with princes.' The ash-heap as the lowest social location from which YHWH lifts his faithful ones is the OT's canonical image of divine reversal — social and economic inversion as YHWH's self-expression.
8: 'For the pillars of the earth are YHWH's, and on them he has set the world' (kî lYHWH mĕṣûqê ʾereṣ wayyāšeṯ ʿălêhem tēḇēl). mĕṣûqê ʾereṣ (H4690, pillars/pegs of the earth) — the cosmological foundations that support the world. The cosmic grounding of the social reversal is the prayer's key insight: YHWH can reverse human fortunes because he owns and controls the foundations of creation. The one who set the world on its pillars can lift a person from the ash heap to the prince's seat — both acts are expressions of the same sovereign power operating at different scales of reality.
9: 'He will guard the feet of his faithful ones, but the wicked shall be cut off in darkness' (raglê ḥăsîḏāyw yišmōr wĕrĕšāʿîm baḥōšeḵ yiddāmmû). ḥăsîḏāyw (H2623, his faithful/covenant-loyal ones) — plural of ḥāsîḏ, one who practices hesed. 'For not by strength shall a man prevail' (kî lōʾ ḇĕkoaḥ yiḡbar ʾîš). This summary principle dissolves the apparent security of the strong: human strength is never the decisive factor in YHWH's economy. The strong fall not because of a rival's greater strength but because YHWH does not honor self-reliance. This theological axiom grounds every reversal in the prayer: the source of outcomes is YHWH's initiative, not human capacity.
10: 'He will give strength to his king and exalt the horn of his anointed' (wĕyārēm qeren mĕšîḥô). The climax of Hannah's prayer is the first use of māšîaḥ (H4899, his anointed one) in Samuel. At this point in the narrative there is no king in Israel — Hannah prays for a future king and anointed one in the same breath as describing YHWH's reversals. The prayer thus looks beyond Samuel (YHWH's immediate answer to Hannah's prayer) to the king YHWH will establish (the structural answer to Israel's need) — and beyond Israel's historical kings to the ultimate māšîaḥ. Acts 2:36 ('God has made him both Lord and Christ') and Rev 12:10 ('the kingdom of our God and the authority of his Christ') are the full realization of this first māšîaḥ invocation.
11: Elkanah returns to Ramah; Samuel ministers before YHWH under Eli. mĕšārēt (H8334, to minister/serve in sanctuary context) — the technical term for Levitical and priestly ministry (Num 8:22; Deut 10:8). Samuel, an Ephraimite rather than a Levite, ministers at the central sanctuary — his role transcends ordinary tribal categories, anticipating the prophetic office that will also transcend tribal boundaries. The contrast is established: Samuel ministers before YHWH; Eli's sons (about to be introduced) minister for themselves.
12: Eli's sons are bĕnê bĕliyyāʿal (sons of worthlessness/Belial) who 'did not know YHWH' (lōʾ yāḏĕʿû ʾet-YHWH). yāḏaʿ (H3045, to know) in the covenantal sense means relational, personal knowledge — the knowledge that comes from covenant relationship. The same verb will describe Samuel's condition in 3:7 ('Samuel did not yet know YHWH') — but the situations are opposite: Eli's sons do not know YHWH due to spiritual corruption; Samuel does not yet know YHWH because YHWH has not yet revealed himself to him. Same surface description, opposite diagnoses. The sons' not-knowing is culpable; Samuel's not-knowing is circumstantial.
13: The priests' sin is technically precise: a three-pronged fork (mazlēg H4207) used to take from the boiling sacrificial pot before the fat was burned to YHWH. Lev 3:3-5 and 7:29-34 specify that the fat must be burned to YHWH first and the breast and right thigh given to the priest. Hophni and Phinehas are taking before YHWH's portion — inverting the sacrificial order and claiming priority over YHWH. This is the specific violation: not taking an unauthorized amount but taking at the wrong time, before the sacred portion designated for YHWH. The timing violation is the theological content of the sin.
14: The three-pronged fork's arbitrary catch — 'all that the fork brought up, the priest took' — reflects the opportunistic character of the sin. No prescribed portion is being observed; the priests take whatever they can get. The verb kōhēn (acting as priest) used in v13 is deeply ironic: those who exercise the priestly office are doing so for self-service. The corruption of sacred office — using covenant functions as vehicles for personal enrichment — is the OT's consistent warning about the danger that attaches to positions of sacred privilege (cf. Ezek 34 on self-serving shepherds).
15: The sin escalates: even before the fat was burned (bĕṭerem yaqṭirûn ʾet-haḥēleḇ), the priest's servant demands raw meat rather than boiled. The preference for raw over cooked meat is a quality preference — roasted meat is superior to boiled. The progression: improper timing (before YHWH's portion is burned) → improper method (raw for roasting rather than the prescribed cooked portion). The escalation pattern is the OT's description of how sin in religious office compounds: first a small violation of timing, then an escalation to a quality preference, then (v16) outright coercion. Sin in sacred office follows an escalating logic.
16: If the worshiper protests — 'burn the fat first, then take as much as you wish' — the servant's response is coercive: 'No, you must give it now; if not, I will take it by force' (wĕʾim-lōʾ lāqaḥtî ḇĕḥāzāqāh). ḥāzāq (H2388, force/strength) applied to the taking of sacrificial portions is the language of extortion. The priest who should facilitate the worshiper's offering to YHWH is now threatening force against the worshiper. This is the inversion of the priestly role from covenant mediator to covenant predator — using the authority of the priestly office as a coercive instrument against the very people the priest exists to serve.
17: 'The sin of the young men was very great in the sight of YHWH, for the men treated the offering of YHWH with contempt' (kî nîʾăṣû hāʾănāšîm ʾet-minḥat YHWH). nāʾaṣ (H5006, to despise/contemn) — used in Num 14:23 for those who treated YHWH with contempt in the wilderness. The 'offering of YHWH' (minḥat YHWH) is the vehicle of the contempt: by taking before YHWH's portion and by using coercion, the priests make the worship system express contempt toward YHWH rather than honor. The narrator's theological precision: the gravity of the sin is not violation of a procedure but the contempt it expresses toward YHWH himself. Contempt for the sacred is contempt for the Holy One.
18: Samuel ministers before YHWH as a naʿar (youth) wearing a linen ephod (ʾēpôḏ bāḏ). The ʾēpôḏ (H646) was primarily the high-priestly garment (Exod 28:6-14) associated with the Urim and Thummim oracle-function (Exod 28:30). Samuel wearing the ephod at this young age — though he is not a Levite — foreshadows his prophetic-oracular role. The contrast with Eli's sons is stark: they take meat for themselves; Samuel wears the vestment of priestly ministry. The linen ephod as the badge of genuine covenant service (cf. 2 Sam 6:14 where David wears it) marks Samuel as the growing alternative to the corrupt priestly establishment.
19: Hannah makes a little robe (mĕʿîl qāṭōn) for Samuel each year. mĕʿîl (H4598, robe/outer garment) — the same word for Samuel's prophetic robe in his adult ministry (15:27; 28:14). The annual garment is Hannah's ongoing investment in Samuel despite his absence: the mother who gave her son to YHWH remains connected to him through the yearly robe. The robe tracks his growth — each year a new robe, each year larger. Hannah's faithfulness to Samuel mirrors YHWH's faithfulness to Hannah: both continue their covenant relationship across distance.
20: Eli's blessing on Elkanah and Hannah — 'May YHWH give you children by this woman in place of the one she asked for YHWH' (taḥat haššĕʾēlāh ʾăšer šāʾal lYHWH) — uses šāʾal again: 'in place of the requesting she asked.' The blessing-language of substitution (taḥat, in place of/instead of) is significant: Hannah's gift to YHWH will be replaced by YHWH's gift to Hannah. The exchange is not equal — Samuel was given once; YHWH will give five more. This is the economics of Prov 11:24-25: 'One gives freely yet grows all the richer.' YHWH's reciprocal giving exceeds the human giving that initiated it.
21: YHWH visits (pāqaḏ, H6485 — the covenant verb of divine initiative, as in 1:19) Hannah and she conceives three more sons and two daughters. The five additional children approaching the seven of Hannah's prayer (v5) represent YHWH's abundant answer. Samuel 'grew' (wayyiḡdal šĕmûʾēl) with YHWH (ʿim YHWH) — the presence of YHWH characterizes his formation. The brief notice of Samuel's growth between the narratives of Eli's corrupt sons and the oracle against Eli's house (vv22-36) keeps the alternative before the reader: while Eli's house disintegrates, YHWH's chosen servant grows.
22: Eli confronts his sons: 'they lay with the women who were serving at the entrance to the tent of meeting' (ʾet-hannāšîm haṣṣōḇĕʾôt petaḥ ʾōhel môʿēḏ). These women were mentioned in Exod 38:8 as performing some function at the tabernacle entrance. The violation of sanctuary servants by the priests is a desecration of both sanctuary and persons. The two violations together — theft from the altar (vv13-17) and sexual exploitation of sanctuary staff (v22) — constitute total inversion of the priestly calling: the priest who should guard the sacred space and protect the vulnerable has become both thief and predator.
23: Eli hears reports (šōmēaʿ ʾet-dĕḇārêhem) from all the people and verbally rebukes his sons — but does not remove them from office. This gap between hearing/knowing and acting to remove is the narrator's critique of Eli's leadership. The information is available; the authority is present; the will is absent. 3:13 will render the explicit verdict: 'his sons were blaspheming God, and he did not restrain them (wĕlōʾ kihāh bām).' The verb kāhāh (H3543, to restrain/rebuke/grow dim) connects Eli's failure to restrain his sons to his own 'growing dim' — the same root for both his ethical and physical weakness.
24: 'It is no good report that I hear the people of YHWH spreading about' — maʿăḇîrîm ʿam-YHWH (the people of YHWH spreading it). The šĕmûʿāh (H8052, report/fame) of YHWH's sanctuary in the community is at stake. The priests who should cause YHWH's name to be honored among his people are causing evil reports to spread instead. Ezek 36:20-23 diagnoses Israel's exile as the consequence of this same dynamic: profaning YHWH's name among the peoples. The priestly function of maintaining YHWH's honor in the community's speech is the inverse of what Hophni and Phinehas are producing.
25: 'If someone sins against a man, God can mediate (wĕpillel lô ʾĕlōhîm); but if someone sins against YHWH, who can intercede?' (mî yitpallel-lô). The root pālal (H6419) appears in both verbs: God mediates/arbitrates (piel: to serve as mediator) human-human conflicts; but for sin against YHWH there is no higher court of appeal. The distinction is crucial: the sins of Hophni and Phinehas are not merely against worshipers but against YHWH himself — they have stolen his portions and desecrated his house. 'But they would not listen to their father's voice, because YHWH desired to kill them' (kî ḥāpēṣ YHWH lahămîtām) — the divine will in their judicial hardening: YHWH's decision to judge them is the reason the rebuke cannot reach them.
26: 'Now the young man Samuel continued to grow both in stature and in favor with YHWH and with men' (wĕhannāʿar šĕmûʾēl hōlēḵ wĕḡāḏōl wāṭôḇ gam ʿim-YHWH wĕgam ʿim-ʾănāšîm). The formula of double-favor (with YHWH and with men) appears in Luke 2:52 for Jesus: 'And Jesus increased in wisdom and in stature and in favor with God and man.' Both Samuel and Jesus are described with this double-alignment formula. The OT use establishes the pattern: the covenant servant who grows in YHWH's estimation and in human recognition is the model for the one who will perfectly embody this double-favor in the messianic age.
27: An unnamed 'man of God' (ʾîš hāʾĕlōhîm) delivers an oracle against Eli's house. The designation 'man of God' is used for prophets throughout the historical books (Moses in Deut 33:1; the unnamed prophet in Judg 13:6; Elijah in 1 Kgs 17:18; Elisha throughout 2 Kgs). The oracle begins with YHWH's prior revelation to the Aaronic priesthood in Egypt — 'niglōh niglêṯî' (I revealed — did I indeed reveal?) — the doubly emphatic infinitive absolute stressing the certainty and completeness of the original revelation. The oracle frames the judgment as covenant violation: YHWH gave extraordinary privilege; extraordinary privilege has been extraordinarily corrupted.
28: The threefold catalog of YHWH's gifts to Aaron's house: (1) chosen from all tribes; (2) altar access; (3) incense-burning. Plus: wearing the ephod; eating the fire offerings. Each item represents a layer of covenant privilege — access to the divine presence, mediation, oracular equipment, and sacrificial sustenance. The catalog's comprehensiveness makes the corruption more indictable: every dimension of covenant privilege has been turned into a vehicle for self-enrichment. The inventory of gift before the verdict of abuse is the OT's standard covenant-lawsuit pattern: 'I gave you everything; you turned everything into something for yourselves.'
29: 'Why do you scorn my sacrifices... and honor your sons above me?' (wattĕkabbēḏ ʾet-bānêḵā mimmennî). The verb kāḇēḏ (H3513, to honor/glorify — the root of kāḇôḏ, glory) used here and in v30: Eli has honored (kāḇēḏ) his sons more than he honored YHWH (mimmennî). The sin is one of disordered honor: giving to his sons the weight of priority that belongs to YHWH. This is the specific violation of the first commandment at the household level — functionally placing his sons before YHWH in the order of his decisive actions. The sin of inaction is the same as actively choosing his sons over YHWH.
30: 'Those who honor me I will honor; those who despise me shall be lightly esteemed' (kî mĕkabbĕḏay ʾăkabbēḏ ûḇōzay yēqāllû). This is the covenant honor-economy's most explicit statement: the kāḇēḏ (honor/weight) you give to YHWH is the measure of what you receive in return. bāzāh (H959, to despise/treat as contemptible) → qālal (H7043, to make light/contemptible). The reversal applies: Eli's sons who 'despised' (nāʾaṣ, v17) YHWH's offerings will themselves be made contemptible. The law of sowing and reaping applied to the honor economy of covenant relationship.
31: 'I will cut off your arm (zĕrôʿ) and the arm of your father's house, so that there will not be an old man in your house' — zĕrôʿ (H2220, arm/strength/line) means both physical power and genealogical line. The cutting off of zĕrôʿ combines the end of physical power and the end of genealogical continuation. Near fulfillment: the Nob massacre (22:18-19) kills 85 priests. Ultimate fulfillment: Solomon deposes Abiathar (1 Kgs 2:26-27), fulfilling 'I will cut off your arm from the altar.'
32: Eli's house will see the prosperity that comes to Israel through the faithful priest — the distress of the sanctuary will be witnessed by the deposed priestly house while YHWH's purposes succeed through another line. The judgment is not only on the guilty generation but on the lineage: the covenant privilege given to Aaron's house cannot be inherited by those who corrupt it. The revision of the eternal promise in v30 ('I promised... but now: far be it from me') demonstrates the conditional nature of priestly privilege — YHWH will fulfill his covenant through a faithful priest, but not through this house.
33: One survivor is preserved to weep — lĕḵallôt ʾet-ʿênāyw ûlĕdaʾăḇ ʾet-napšô (to wear out his eyes and to grieve his soul). The partial preservation is more bitter than total elimination: the survivor witnesses the complete fulfillment of the oracle without being able to prevent any of it. Fulfilled in Abiathar (1 Kgs 2:26-27), who is exiled to Anathoth and lives to see the Zadokite priesthood replace his line in Solomon's temple. The survivor who witnesses the judgment is the living sign of the oracle's complete fulfillment.
34: 'Both of them shall die on the same day' — the simultaneous death of Hophni and Phinehas as a covenant sign (ʾôt, H226). ʾôt is the same word for the circumcision sign (Gen 17:11) and the Exodus signs — a divine marker that the event is a judgment-act, not historical accident. The precise prediction (same day) makes the fulfillment unambiguous: when both sons die on the same day in 4:11, the entire oracle's reliability is confirmed. The sign-character of their simultaneous death establishes Samuel's prophetic authority — YHWH speaks through him what YHWH then fulfills exactly.
35: 'I will raise up for myself a faithful priest who shall do according to what is in my heart and in my mind' (kōhēn neʾĕmān kăʾăšer bilĕḇāḇî ûḇĕnapšî yāʿăśeh). neʾĕmān (H539, faithful/trustworthy — from ʾāman, the root of 'amen') characterizes the promised priest. The criterion of faithfulness — acting 'according to what is in my heart and in my mind' rather than what serves personal interest — is the inversion of Hophni and Phinehas who acted according to their own appetites. The near fulfillment is Zadok; the ultimate fulfillment is Christ as great high priest who acts entirely according to the Father's will (Heb 5:7-10: he learned obedience through what he suffered and was appointed high priest forever).
36: The survivors of Eli's house will come to bow before the faithful priest for a piece of silver or a morsel of bread — the descendants of the priests who stole sacrificial portions will beg for scraps. The inversion is precise: they took more than their portion by force (v16); they will receive far less than their portion by begging. The oracle closes the covenant-lawsuit with the pattern of complete reversal: what was taken out of place will be needed in diminishment. The faithful priest who receives the transferred privilege must do 'what is in YHWH's heart and mind' — the only basis on which covenant office can be legitimately held.
